loss_record.structural_divergence: Java encodes identity as Function.identity() (static factory) or x -> x (lambda); both preserve type T through generics <T>; abstraction is silent on implementation; method reference vs lambda is a stylistic choice with no semantic difference
loss_record.domain_narrowing: Java 8+ required for Function<T, T> and lambda expressions; pre-Java 8 requires manual Function subclass or equivalent; realization narrows domain to Java 8+ environments
loss_record.ub_introduction: none: Function.identity() and lambda x -> x are pure safe operations; no memory unsafety, no null dereferences; argument nullability is type-dependent and preserved from abstraction
loss_record.effect_divergence: none: Function.identity() and lambda expressions are pure; no side effects, I/O, or mutable state
loss_record.value_divergence: Java's Function<T, T> is a nominal interface type with one method apply(T): T; Rust's identity is a structural function item with zero size; Java realization depends on JDK Function interface and runtime polymorphism; Rust realization is compile-time function with zero runtime overhead
